Hi, welcome back to module five of Straight Line Persuasion. This is the inner game of sales. And this is crucially important here because what happens very often is that we tend to focus on our results in the exterior world, in the real world, and we don't focus on what happens up here. And in fact, success starts inside your mind. You have to condition your mind for success, to condition your mind to move through obstacles, through fears, and to focus on where you want to go in life and not what you're afraid of. See so many people out there, they have a tendency to live in the problem versus in the solution. So what we're doing here in this module is we're giving you some very powerful tactics, some very powerful techniques that will allow you to develop a laser guided focus on your outcome, on where you want to go. And you're going to learn some words that are very destructive, things like the word should and the word problems that tend to disempower people. And you're going to learn a process called reframing, where you actually can take those words and apply a new meaning to them that propel you to taking action versus keep you in a box and stop you from taking action. I will tell you this, there's a lot of metaphors that I use in this module and I want you to focus on the metaphors because what they really do is they're going to link up your past experiences in your own life and allow you to really see how without knowing it, your mind has worked against you and stopped you from being all that you can be, not just in sales and persuasion, but in your relationships, maybe with your children, whatever it might be socially and especially financially. So again, this module here, it's crucial. You're going to learn very basic tools here but that are very, very powerful. They're all going to be easy to use, which is very important. And before you watch it, I want you to remember one thing here and you're going to hear this in the module and see it on the DVD, that you are not the mistakes of your past. You are the resources and the capabilities that you've gleaned from your past. That if anything is the most crucial distinction in this DVD. And I've used this by the way when I worked with a lot of kids who are for charity with the Salvation Army. Kids who had terrible, terrible upbringings and had insult after insult dealt with them by their parents, by society. Many are addicted to drugs, they're living on the streets. And when I get these kids in a room for a couple of hours and I work with them closely, the core message I get through to them is that everything that happened in your past that was an insult or that hurt you, it now becomes muscle for you to go forward. Get that learning from the past and to get tough, to be able to go forward right now, to move through fear, to move through uncertainty and to move through self-doubt most of all, to keep your eye focused on where you want to go. So remember, keep your action book handy, make notes, watch for the metaphors related to your own life and I'll see you on the other side. I talked about Bobby Koch, okay? The guy who had an IQ of officer of a forest gump, right? He became a millionaire. He's a fabulous guy. And what Bobby Koch really exemplified, that trait he has, which you could call your standards in life, the standard you hold yourself through, what you will not settle for less than, okay? It's based on this, okay? You'd be willing to do what other people are unwilling to do today, today, to get what you want tomorrow. Most will tell you they want to get successful, they want to make money, they want to get ahead in life, but they're not willing to go the extra yard. I told you a couple of stories before and it was in there, embedded in there, was the secret to success. I said to you that when I was 40 years old and lost my money, I went out and knocked on doors until my knuckles bled. No one else was going to do that. I'm sure some of you were, but where I was, no one would do it. So I cornered the market in that and I made a fortune. When I decided I wanted to become a speaker, I holed up in my house, just so you know, and not about this stuff, the straight line is I'm unconsciously competent. I can teach this in my sleep. But I'm talking about other stuff, the wealth creation on a different level, right, when speaking to entrepreneurs and I had things I needed to learn, own my own skills. I worked for months and months and months and months nonstop, reading and reading and reading and writing and writing and writing. I developed my own language patterns because I knew that if I wanted to be really truly successful at the highest level, I had to be the best and especially the best I could be. Now I'm bearing the fruits of that. But people say, oh my God, you just started this business. You started to be a public speaker last year and you're on this massive roll call. No, I'm not. I didn't start this business last year. Hey, I started when I was 20 years old or 16 years old hustling ices on the beach. Who here has been a hustle since they were a teenager? Raise your hand. I bet you a lot of you are. Exactly. Okay, I know. I was out sharing war stories in my class and he's telling me stories. He's hustling, gold watches, rolling through 16 years old. He's out there in Canal Street. I get it. I get it. That's why he's successful. That's why you're successful. That's why you're successful. Okay? You're doing to do what it takes. Caveat, that's ethical. Doesn't harm somebody else. That's productive to humanity. You do whatever it takes to become successful. So I'm going to teach you now. We're going to go into the straight line, okay, the actual mechanics of it and the language patterns. Okay? And I want you to understand that for some of you, it's going to take some work because you have your language skills are a little bit lower down on the totem pole right now. You're not natural clothes. Some things might take time, but if you want to be successful and I promise you, so if you're the sort of person that's going to struggle a bit, don't worry. The beauty is in the struggle. The beauty is in the struggle. Once you get there, it's great. You enjoy it, but the beauty is in the struggle. That's the way life is. I told you the first word that's the most destructive in the English language was what? Should, right? Should. What should you, when you should, you should all over yourself. That's what you do. Okay? It's the procrastinating favor. I should have went out. I should have done my taxes. Oh, should I got to do my taxes? I should have done this. I should have done that. And they don't do anything. That should. The second most destructive word in the English language is this. I'll write it up here. See it. Do this in blue now. Problems. Everybody's got a freaking problem. Problem, problem, problem. Everyone's got problems, right? What a load of crap this word is. Problems. Wipe this word out of your language patterns, okay? And replace it with this word. Challenges. That's the word. Problems is disempowering and overwhelms you. Challenges you meet head on and you rise up to them and you overcome them. Another word for this? Obstacles. Barriers. Whatever is standing in your way from you and your goal and your vision. Change the word problems to challenges. Remember I said to you before earlier, you know, who has more problems? Rich people or poor people, right? Poor people is like, well, poor people. I know they don't. Rich people have more problems because they've got a lot more shit going on. And there's always a problem. There's always a problem when you have money. It's just less painful to handle it. The real word that empowers you is challenges. Look at it this way. And I know most of you are successful, but some of you probably right now are struggling financially. It's been a tough time in the economy and some of you are on the way you're bouncing back, okay? And one of the things I say to groups of people, especially when they're struggling, when I'm doing a seminar, it's about wealth creation, there's a lot of people that really want to be entrepreneurs and they're not there yet. And they're so caught up in the muck and mire of their past. In their past mistakes, their past failures. And they're so invested in that and they just don't think of themselves in terms of being a guy or a girl who could be successful. And let me just tell you something. Here is the real deal. If there's one thing that you get in this for non-sales, it's this. Just for your life. You, you, and you, you are not the mistakes of your past. You are not the mistakes of your past. You are the resources and the capabilities you've gleaned from it. There's a huge difference. As soon as you make that shift in your life, you're going to realize that anything that's happened to you bad is your greatest asset. I was interviewed on TV in New Zealand. You can, it's online, by the way, you can find it, like last week. And I'm on being interviewed by this guy and there's a great interview from New Zealand TV. The last question, it goes, you know, you're making millions and millions of dollars right now and you know, in some way, don't you think, are you saying you're glamorizing that, you know, you can, you know, commit a crime and now make millions and millions of dollars. You wrote best sellers, you're on a tour and this was not an adversarial interview. This was a very friendly interview. He asked me a genuine question. It wasn't like, are you, you know, he was very nice about it. And I, you know, I was kind of stunned for a second because I hadn't thought about it. And I said, absolutely not. I said, my past is my greatest asset. What happened to me, now I'm proud of what I did and I feel bad. I have remorse, remorse, not guilt. Guilt is a loser's emotion. Remorse is the emotion that allows you to move forward and do things right. Guilt is like, oh, I feel so guilty. I'm always me and you want sympathy on poor you. That's bullshit. Remorse is the empowering emotion. My past is my greatest asset. My past has allowed me to A, have the credibility to stand up in any room and know that I don't just talk to talk, but I've walked the walk, that I've made mistakes. I represented people that are down at the bottom, no matter how far, how low you've fallen on the totem pole of life, of relationships, of love, anything. I lost everything, not just my money. I lost my wife. She walked out the door the day I got indicted, guys. The day I was walking down the courthouse steps and she said, I wanted divorce. Imagine that. Not that I was a model husband or anything while I was married to her. Those who read my book know that, but my point is this. One day, I lost my freedom, my money, and my family. I wanted to crawl up in a hole and die. I wanted to crawl up in a hole and die. If you're in a spot in your life right now, I hope you're not, but if you are, I want to tell you something. There is no level, no matter how low it is, that you can't come back from, like gangbusters, because I did it. I'm living proof of it. I'm living proof. I came back harder and harder than I ever was at in a way that empowers other people, helps people, where I'm now an article called The Guru of Antigreed in the biggest newspaper in Australia. You know how honestly, the first person I showed it to, you know, was my children. I said, look at your dad. I said, look at this. I said, read this article. They called me The Guru of Antigreed. And I was proud, more than the money, more than any fame I might get. When I saw that, I had to show it to my son and my daughter. That's what it's all about. So I don't care where you are in life. If you're not where you want to be, that's okay. You're not your past. You're the resources and capabilities that you've gleaned from it. Make sense, everybody? Yes or yes? Newton's law and object and motion tends to stay in motion. What that means is as follows. If you are in a bad spot right now, if you're skidding in a bad direction right now, you're going to keep skidding there. It doesn't stop like that. An object in motion will stay in motion. Imagine this. I'll tell you a story. I used to fly a lot of helicopters. And when I took my license, I had to do something called auto rotations. Anybody here fly helicopters at all? Auto rotations is when if the helicopter engine dies, you can land it without an engine by allowing the helicopter to go into a controlled dive. And then the last second you pull up on the stick and use the stored energy and the helicopter flares and you skid to a safe but bumpy landing. That's auto rotation. So my captain said, he was a wild man, my captain, because he was also the captain of my yacht, which we actually sank as we drove it out into a storm. He says, listen, here's the deal. Auto rotation is simple. You're going to dive down at a 45 degree angle with no engine. And at 50 feet, you're going to pull back at the stick. But the key is, do not look at the ground. You got to look forward to where you want to go. You look forward with your eyes, with your focus, and your body will take you there. I was like, OK, that's great. That's what I teach people in seminars. Like, you know, focus on where you want to go and you'll get there, right? Great. And then we had to turn the engine off. We had to practice this. I was like, what a stupid idea this is. Can't we do it in a simulator? No, you can't do it in a simulator. You've got to do it for real. So we go up and I'm like, no problem. I'm strong. I'm tough. We go out there. He turns the engine off. We start diving down at 50 feet. I start pulling back. I'm like, holy shit, holy shit. And I'm looking at the ground. I can't take my eyes off the ground. And I'm going, he takes my tinny, yanked it up so I look forward. I pull back on the stick and I go to a safe, but bumpy landing. Ironically, I knew not to focus on what I was scared of, but that's human nature. We focus on what we're afraid of and we crash into it because on what we focus on is where we go. Where focus goes, energy flows. What you focus on expands in your life. So if you're in a skid right now, here's the metaphor for your life. If you're not going where you want to go right now, don't say, oh my God, things are so bad. Things are so bad. Boom. You crash right into it. You set a goal and you look at the goal, not the problem. You don't invest yourself in the problem you invest in the solution and you keep looking at it. Now, what happened is this, when the helicopter was diving at 50 feet and I pulled back on the stick and I looked ahead, did it continue to dive? Yes, it had momentum. Get it? An object in motion will stay in the same motion. It has momentum. So my instinct goes, shit, it ain't working. I want to see the hit. It's like when you want to get punched, let me at least see the punch coming so you want to see the accident before it actually happens. Human nature. Right? You want to look at it. Well, it's not going to crash. I'm going to say, look, ah, you ever see a guy? This isn't the business. There's always a guy in a Ferrari, some stupid rich guy in Westchester. He's got a Ferrari and it's a wide open road and it's fields everywhere and he crashed into the one telephone pole. Like how the hell did that happen? Guess what? Same thing, where focus goes. He's like, oh my God, a telephone pole. He's like, no, no, right into the telephone pole. What do you think happens when you're focusing on the crap in your life? What you focus on expands. What you focus on, you attract. So let me say this, if you're focusing on all the crap in your life, what do you become? A giant crap magnet. You attract crap to you and it gets bigger and bigger until you fall in an avalanche of it and get destroyed. So here's the irony. So he tells me this and I'm like, geez, I knew to look up ahead but I couldn't do it. I said, well, let's do it again. I had to do it again. We went back up again. We started going around the thing, making a pad and we get to the bottom. He turns up the engine. We start diving down. I'm feeling good. I'm like, holy shit, again, I'm still looking down. He pushes my chin up. I pull back on the stick and sure enough, I go to a safe landing. Why would I still look down knowing that I shouldn't? Because I'm conditioned. We are conditioned to look at our problems and to focus on our problems and because of that, we were paralyzed with fear and we crashed right into them. So bankrupt, lose only love, get sick, whatever it might be, we get destroyed. Third time up, I had to do it again. They had my license, right? He takes me in, pad and we dive down 50 feet, get the 50 feet. I pull back and I look up. I felt like looking down but I looked up. Why? Because I conditioned a new pattern. You can break your old patterns. Step one is interrupt them. That's what I'm trying to do right now is interrupt your pattern. If you're one of these people who focus on crapping your life, I'm interrupting your pattern right now. Step two is condition a new pattern. Now, just start looking at your solutions on your goals and on your vision. Doesn't matter where you've been. Imagine if you're on the speedboat and you look behind you at the wake. Ask yourself, does that foamy white thing have anything to do with where I'm going now? Doesn't the wake does not drive the boat? Your wake is your past. What drives the boat is you, the captain, and where you're focusing on, right? Your focus, that's where you're going. And the engine, the motor, which is what? That's your actions. Okay? The state that you're in, this resourceful state to be taking action and focusing on the good stuff versus the crap. It's called being a, not a creature of circumstance, but a creator of circumstance. You set a goal, you focus on it, laser-guided, like the straight line. When someone walks in the door of your salesperson on the phone, your focus is on the close. Everything else is all the noise in between. It's life. So you condition a new pattern and you move forward. It's as simple as that. At the heart of it all, and I'm going to move on past this stuff, self-development, at the heart of it all, but I need to give you this stuff too, because it's really important for sales, and such an up-and-down business, and one day it's great, next day it's a disaster. And I want to just share some distinctions with you that can help you manage the way you feel as a salesperson, as a human being, moment to moment. Because what a state does, your state, I'm talking about your physiological, emotional state, how you feel now. There are states that lead to wealth and success. I said a couple before about sales, certainty, clarity, conviction, confidence, and your personal life are those of the best states, maybe not. Maybe it's love, compassion, understanding, empathy. Those are very empowering states. Master the ability to be in those states and you got something. Conversely, there's disempowering states, which are fear, jealousy, greed, and on the opposite, ambition would be the empowering state, right? Jealousy, greed, overwhelm, uncertainty. Those states lead you to A, perform at a low level, B, to be in more unresourced states, because they almost snowball on each other. You know when you're on a roll as a salesperson and you're just doing great, you're like, I'm on a roll, right? That's called resourceful state, yielding a resourceful state, yielding a resourceful state. When you're feeling like crap as a salesperson and you can't close a door, it's because damn I can't close, I damn I can't close, and you'll walk in. I'll tell you a great story about state management. My first day ever training for sales, I was 21 years old and it was door-to-door sales for meat and seafood, as I said. My first day on the job, my friend trained me. I was in Florida, very successful guy, very successful. He was selling on a pickup truck, those guys that go around selling steaks and shrimps and lobstails on boxes. He takes me around his door-to-door cold-cancing, the worst job in the world. Really awful job. Takes me around and knocks at like 20 doors in the morning. Each door is like, hey, how you doing? My name is Elliot. I deliver Flavio and Trip, blah, blah, blah, all upbeat and positive. I'm looking at this thing and everyone's like, no, and then I should know slamming the door in his face. Somewhere around 12.30, I walk up to the door and he opens the door. I see his morning, his shoulders are getting slightly down and down. He's not negative yet. Almost like 12.30, he knocks on the door and they open the door. He goes like, you wouldn't want any Flavio and Trip and lobsters, right? I delivered to some of the other people and no one wants today. Do you want any? That's how his rap degenerated. He went in a state of complete and utter uncertainty. You can't close. You can't open. You can't do anything as a salesperson if you're in that state where you don't think they're going to buy. You cannot do anything meaningful in that state. I watched it before my eyes, a salesperson. You must see this, especially if you're a sales manager and you're sitting in an office and you see someone that's not doing well and you watch and they just sink and sink and sink and they pick up the phone like, hi, it's so-and-so calling from XYZ company. You really wouldn't want any of my product, would you? I'm not going to close a lot like that. That's the ability to manage your state. It's how you act in the moment. You might be the best salesman in the world, but how are you right now? Can you close right now? You might be the best parent in the world, but if you're in a state of impatience, how great of a parent can you be? You might have read all the books on parenting. You might love your kids to death, but if you're in a disempowered state of impatience and overwhelm, can you parent? Yes or no? Can't. You might be the greatest closer in the world, but you're in a state of absolute uncertainty. Can you close? Uh-uh. They pick on that up in a second. They pick up on it in a second. The state you're in in the moment dictates your success or failure. There's one thing in life, a distinction that can really get you ahead. It's the ability to manage your state. Bar none. That's number one. How our brain makes sense of the world, okay? We do three things. Okay? In this room right now, you think you're seeing the whole room, but you're not. What you're doing is you're deleting, distorting, and generalizing everything around you to make sense of the world. Write that down. You delete, distort, and generalize. I'm going to give you a very quick exercise here so you can see the power of this. And I want everybody right now to close their eyes and when your eyes close, because everyone close your eyes for this exercise, just for a couple seconds. And I want, with your eyes closed, I want you to clear it out completely so you see nothing, just enough to go for blankness right now. Okay? Okay? Okay? Now, I want you to open up your eyes. Okay? And I want you to look around the room for blue. Look for blue. Look for blue. Look for blue. Look for blue. Look for blue. Now, you can see that you can possibly find in this room. Look for blue. Look for blue. Look for more blue. Now, stop or close your eyes. Okay? With your eyes closed, I want you to think of everything in the room that's red. So, I bet you see a lot more blue right now than red, right? Now, open your eyes and now start looking for red. Look for red. Look for red. Look for red. Look for red. Look for more red. Okay, great. You see a lot more red now that you're looking for it. Carpet's red, right? Her shirt is red, right? The exit sign is red. The Coca-Cola, the bottom of the top, the top is red, right? Anyone else see any red? Who sees red? The chair is red. Okay? Anything else red? Anything else see red? Got the carpet. What else? Anything else is red in the room? Keep going. Someone has got to be more red in this room. Exit sign said that. Anything else is red? Okay, there we go. Bingo! Okay, he said it. He said the chair is red. Is the chair really red though? Barely. It's almost pinkish the chair. But because he's looking for it, you know what happens? You start making shit up to be right. It happens every time. It's really more pink than red. It's not red. It's almost, maybe it's red. It's like, why not? Red? Sure, I'm looking for it. So let me ask you this. What happens when you start looking for problems? You'll start making them up if they ain't even there. That's the power of deletion. Conversely, what happens when you're focusing on your goal and your vision? You start seeing every opportunity that comes your way. If you're out there looking for all the crap on the problem, the opportunities fly over your head every single time. You delete them out. There's a portion of your brain called the RAS, the Reticular Activating System. And what it does, it decides what you focus on and what you ignore. Because you can't focus on everything around you. There's too much data. Right now you're focusing on me, maybe that, maybe the person you're right, everything else. You're not feeling it. Feeding of your heart and your artery over here by your ear. You don't hear the hum of the air conditioner. Now you do. I ignite your RAS. You don't hear that wriggling of his paper. Or maybe you did. But if you heard that, you weren't hearing the air conditioner. You can't hear it all. You just can't. Your unconscious mind gets it all. It absorbs it all. Because it's much more powerful than your conscious mind. And you start getting stuff in there. You don't even realize it's there. When you delete out solutions in your life, you're never going to get ahead. That's why setting goals and having vision is so important. So you delete, okay, number one. Second thing, you distort. This is a classic. Distort stuff. I always love what it's men and women fighting with each other. A guy fights with his wife. He calls up his friend. He goes, my wife is the biggest ass in the world. She always hand-packs me. She's always got a problem. She never understands me. Words like always never biggest. We distort the truth to make always. Every human being does this. And then what does your friend say? I know exactly what you mean. They're all like that. They're all. The woman calls up her friend. My husband is the biggest ass. He doesn't see me. He doesn't understand me. Never. Never sees me. Never understands me. I know no men. They're all like that. We do. Men and women. And every... It's both sexes do that. We distort everything. We make the things that are good really good. And things that are bad are really, really bad. We delete. We distort. And lastly, we generalize. We look to make patterns. To understand these three things when you're closing, the reason why I'm saying this, you're going to start to learn how to use this to close. Because when you get people seeing things a certain way, you ignite their RAS, they will delete out the problems. Distort to allow themselves to wash in the positive emotions of your product. We generalize that because you sound good and seem honest, you are. You are. You're not like the rest of them. You generalize. You use these things. It's very powerful to motivate yourself and motivate other people to buy. But again, never do this in a way that disempowers somebody else. Don't get them to falsely associate into a good benefit if it's not really there for them. If it is there, you ought to. You really... You really shame on you. If you don't allow someone to really feel the benefit of a product, you're not doing your job. Shame on you. Likewise, if they're all focused on the negative, you don't move into the positive, no one can live like that in the negative. You do your job, but don't do it in an evil, underhanded way. That's the ship right there that I should have shown earlier. That represents with you at the helm controlling your destiny toward your goal. If that ship leaves Tampa and sets sail for Spain and there's a captain at the helm, what percentage of the time will it reach Spain? Like 99.9, almost always, unless something really bad happens. Now imagine that ship going to Spain without a captain going randomly. What's the chances of reaching its goal? Like zero. It won't happen. I want you to imagine every sail that you make from this point forward has a goal. From the time you start the sail, it has a goal, and the goal is going to be the close. The three ways of managing fear. Number one, pretend it doesn't exist. You ignore it. This is what people who really, really, really live life at the lowest level, they're all employees. They never own a business. They don't sell on commission. They never get ahead in life. They're creatures of utter circumstance. They say, I'm not scared of anything. They don't pretend I'm not scared. No, it's not fear that's holding me back from taking action. It's not fear that stops me from being a commissioned based salesman. It's not fear that stops me from doing business. No, no, no, it's my story. And they have a story about why they don't have what they want in life. And that's what holds them back from getting what they want in life. It's your fear. This is what holds you back. Plain and simple. That's strategy one. They ignore it, and they live a completely disempowered existence. This is the next way to manage fear. You act in spite of fear. This is what I did for many, many years. When I first started, and I still struggle with it today, by the way, I bet most of you in this room probably live this way. You're scared to hell, but you still act. You're scared to do something, you act. You're scared to go up to a roller-ball, but you do it. When you live this way, you become very wealthy, but very miserable. You become anxious. You feel anxiety. Because what's happening is you're scared to do stuff. You're scared about your outcomes. You live in fear, but you act anyway. You don't let it disempower you, but you let it give you those terrible feelings inside of anxiety. I lived this way for many, many years. When I first met my girlfriend, she's like, I've never met a guy like you. You are just not scared of anything in terms of business and success and officials. You're not scared of anything. You go out and do it. I'm like, whoa. I said, no, no. I'm really scared. I just don't want it to stop me. There's a difference. Of course, I'm scared. I just act. That's not the best way to manage fear. The best way to manage fear is this, is to understand it and accept it. This is how you really manage fear and live an empowered life with positive emotions, not just money, but you live your life at a higher emotional level. What that really means, and I say understand it and accept it, what it means is this, is to just accept the fact that most of the fears that you feel are smoke screen, smoke screens for deeper fears. There's two fears that have really run deep for all human beings. I'll tell you what they are. The first is that in some way, on some level, you're not enough. You're just not enough. You're perceived by people that you love and like, you're not enough. You don't measure up. I had that fear my whole life, and I worked really, really, really hard to measure up, which is difficult when you're five foot seven, maybe five foot six and three quarters, but either way, I worked my whole life to measure up because in my mind, I felt like whatever it is, I wasn't enough. I worked really, really hard. I don't know why I felt that, but I just did. I'm just being honest with you. Guess what? We've all felt this way. If you're lying, if you're saying, no, not me, I never, of course you did. The second fear that runs even deeper than not measuring up is that if I don't measure up, I won't be loved or accepted and liked. They won't love me. I really felt that one too. I'm sure at one point in life, but every day, you all have. You develop smoke screens to get around that. It's such a powerful, deep-seated fear. It comes back. You know what it comes back to when we were babies? This is not your fault or my fault. When we were babies, we made a very, very bad generalization. We delete, distort, and generalize. The generalization that we made is we equated attention and love as the same thing. We made that mistake as we were this big. One day, we were crying. We were perfect. They loved us. Our parents loved us. Our babies were perfect. We could poop it out. They loved us. All of a sudden, one day, you weren't that cute anymore. You were like a year and a half, and you cried. Stop it. You're like, what the hell happened here? I had it really good before. Some babies are like, wow, wow, and they still, no one loves them, no one likes them. Then all of a sudden, they fall down, they get hurt, and their mother runs, oh my God. Ah. I would find one of these people, we call them problem people. They know they get attention by having problems. If they hurt really bad, people will love them and they'll get attention. They have problem people. Other people, what happens is they feel neglected. They're not getting that connection right, and all of a sudden, they get up. They start to walk again, and their mother, oh my God, the baby's walking. Look, and an achiever's born. And they get their attention and love through achievement. That's what I did. Okay? Other people, okay, they're babies, they're not getting the attention. They feel that loss of love, and they go out, and they break something. What did you do? They got the connection back. You got people that are always breaking shit. They're broken people, and they get the connection like that. Pleasers, breakers, achievers, problem people. There's a million ways to get the connection and love back. Which one of you? I'm an achiever. We all have our strategies we run to get the connection back, but at the heart of it all is this irrational fear that we won't be loved and we're not enough. When you accept that, when I actually took that into my heart and realized the truth is it doesn't matter whether I succeed or fail. My kids love me. My significant other loves my parents, love me, my friends will love me. Once I got to that point, it allowed me to then operate at the highest level and feel good about it, and not be miserable every single day about my outcome. You follow that concept? Okay, it becomes much different then. Try to take your fears, not overnight. You still struggle there. You remember when you're feeling fearful and you're feeling disempowered. Here's the devil's trick, is the story. It's almost like there's a circuit breaker effect that pops in. It's like you start feeling, feel a bump story. I'm not rich because the economy's bad. That's your story. Okay? I'm not rich because my boss is an asshole. I'm not rich because I wasn't born rich. Guess what? That's what, poor people. They were always rich. No, they weren't. 94% of the rich people in the world started out with nothing. And I'm one of them. I know there's others in this room too. Okay? So it's all excuses. It's all crap. Okay? The system basically works. It's the bottom line. You have to sell yourself, not just on the straight line, but on your own life. Okay? When you focus on your life every day, you look at the successes, not the failures. I won't tell you, and I had dinner with Michael last night and we talked about this. It was very funny. He's like, God, I can't tell you how many failures I've had in my life. He started raffling with like six, like 12 businesses, like fail, fail, fail, fail, fail, fail. But at the end of this, this is two guys talking about it in conceptual terms, right? To understand human performance, right? But I can promise you this, that when I go out into the world and I'm thinking and acting, I'm only remembering my successes. I don't invest myself in my failures. At the end of the day, the successes that you celebrate and you build on them. If you set a goal, I assure you this, and you will set goals today, I assure you that if you set a goal and there's six or seven things required to achieve that goal, you will fail on four of them. You will. And I will use the word failure in a very special way because it's really feedback. But let's just call failure for a second until we reframe that, okay? You'll fail on four of them and two or three will be perfect, right? You focus on what's working. At the end of the day, you build on what's working, not on what's not working. You focus on your successes, you get yourself into a peak state and you move forward. You change your approach for what's not working. And that's how you move forward in life, okay? We're going to set right now, okay, 60-day goals. I want to explain to you exactly what you've got to do here, okay? It's very simple, okay? I want you to set a goal and I want this goal to be specific, specific for the amount of money you want to be earning at that point. I want you to break it down into how many sales you need to be closing to get to the point you want as a salesperson, mostly salespeople here. How many hours a week you're going to be working or a day, how many dials, whatever it is. I want you to get very specific on exactly what you want. I want you to set your goals, I said, not up at the ceiling. You raise your hand as high as you can and just a little bit more. That's where your goal should be. Reach your goal, but stretch. The greatest cause, the number one cause of media awkwardness in this world is not that people set goals that are lofty and miss them. That's not what happens. You set goals that aren't lofty enough and you hit them and you become average. Average sucks. Average doesn't make it in this world. It just doesn't, especially if you're in sales. You have to set your goals high enough so that they're meaningful when you hit them. Relsho said these little goals, they're like, I'm like, go great. Nothing happens. I want you to set goals at that level I spoke about just above where you're comfortable. And I want you now to take it one step further and take that goal and make it into a vision. I want you to get specific. Write down exactly how you see yourself in 60 days from now enjoying your newfound prosperity or your increased prosperity. Who will you be talking to? What will you be saying? How do you feel? Did you buy something new? Is there something in your house you wanted to get fixed? Is there a credit card bill that's overdue that you couldn't pay? I just paid my bill. I want you to imagine yourself at that moment and allow those feelings to wash over you. Write it down. When you write down a goal, here's the system. Number one, you write down a goal. Two, you put a date on it. Your goal has to have a date. Step three, identify the peoples, the groups, and the organizations you need to align yourself with. Step four, you take massive action. Step five, see what's working and what's not and change your approach accordingly. That's it. You set a goal, put a date on it. Identify the peoples, groups, and organizations standing in your way or you need to align yourself with the obstacles. Take massive action. See what's working, see what's not working, and you change your approach. You set a goal, you got to do seven things, you accomplish three. Great. Before it didn't work, what's not working? Tweaking. You don't do the same thing over and over again. That's the definition of insanity. To do the same thing again and again and expect a different result. So I want you to set a goal and I want you to put a date on it. I want you to get specific how you're going to feel in your personal life, what you can buy, and I want you to in there include someone more important to you than yourself, whether it's your children, your spouse or your parents, someone more important to you than yourself, and I want you to write something that you could do for them for achieving this goal. But you'd be no more of this whole thing, you'd be no more than a page. Just one page. I want to make it specific with language. It doesn't matter if you're a good writer or a bad writer. It doesn't matter. Just make it specific for language. No one's going to see this goal button. I'm not going to ask you to read these out loud. They're for you. They're only for you. But what I am going to do is I'm going to ask you to take them and put the bullets on a little card and carry it around with you for the next 60 days. I want you to carry your goals around with you and on top of your goals is going to be your vision. I'm going to ask you to put your goals and your vision on your screensaver for your telephone. So every time you pick up your iPod or your iPhone or your Blackbird, whatever it might be, you see your goal. Because what it does is it ignites your RAS and you start seeing all the red and not the blue. You start seeing all the opportunity and all the positives and not the negatives and you soar. So what do you think of Module 5? For my money, Module 5 is perhaps the most pivotal module of all because that's the one that really gets into selling yourself, your own minds and getting yourself prepared to accept the rest of the information here and to act on it and to feel good about it. To not go through life in that disempowered creature of circumstance state where you really think the world happens to you and to live as a creator of circumstance. To know that the actions that you take on a consistent basis will ultimately yield that result, that laser-guided focus. Remember when we spoke about Newton's law that an object in motion tends to stay in motion? Well, we know that's true. You know, you heard it on the DVD and when you've related hopefully that metaphor to your own life, you've seen times that you were heading down the wrong path yet through simple inertia you just kept walking down that same path. So the idea now is to remember that the most powerful way to break that negative momentum is through massive, directed action. So the goal here and the action steps here, there's two of them, are going to be number one to take one definite precise action tomorrow or if it's still early in the day then you could take it today. And also make sure that that action impacts you in persuasion. Don't make it some random action. I want it to be about what you're learning. Secondly, we're going to set 60-day goals here and get serious about this. Remember, you've got to put a date on your goals, identify the people's groups and organizations you need to align yourself with, any skills you need to learn and then you're going to take that massive, massive action. And when I say massive action, by the way, it could be a baby step because a series of baby steps together yield massive action. But make sure these baby steps are close enough together to break the negative momentum. And if you take the steps, you take the massive action, I want you to analyze your results, see what's working, what's not working and you're going to change your plan accordingly. Shockingly, what you're going to find out is there's not that much that's not working for you when it comes to persuasion. And the reason is, is because you're dealing with a proven system. And that's the beauty of a system. See, rather than having to make all the mistakes yourself and figuring this stuff out, you're dealing with someone like me who's been doing this for, you know, 23 years now, actually 25 years, I'm getting older, and who's trained thousands and thousands of people. And I've seen the struggles that they go through and I've specifically designed and redesigned this system to deal with those struggles, to make it ABC simple, you know, step by step, move by move, to allow you to embrace these techniques so you'll be the best persuader you can possibly be.
